figures.gloss: The founder (1920–2012), born in Jeongju in what is now North Korea, who reported that on Easter 1935 Jesus appeared and asked him to complete his unfinished mission. Imprisoned in the Hungnam labor camp (1948–50) and freed during the UN advance, he founded the church in Seoul in 1954, married Hak Ja Han in 1960 (the 'Marriage of the Lamb'), built a global movement and business-media empire, and in 1992 publicly declared the couple the True Parents and Messiah.
figures.facet: A messiah-claim engineered as completion rather than replacement: Moon positioned himself not against Jesus but as the Lord of the Second Advent finishing what the crucifixion cut short — the claim's force depends entirely on the Christianity it extends.
figures.provenance: Exposition of the Divine Principle (Wolli Kangron, 1966; Eng. 1973); George D. Chryssides, The Advent of Sun Myung Moon (1991); Massimo Introvigne, The Unification Church (2000).

figures.gloss: The doctrinal office Moon and Hak Ja Han are held to occupy: the restored Adam and Eve, the first couple to form a sinless God-centered family, from whom a new lineage free of the Fall descends. Since Moon's death (2012) Hak Ja Han has led the main body as 'True Mother,' a succession contested by rival sons' schismatic churches.
figures.facet: Salvation re-founded on the family unit rather than the individual believer: the messianic office here is inherently a married pair, which makes the wedding — not the cross, not baptism — the tradition's central sacrament.
figures.provenance: Exposition of the Divine Principle, Part II (1966/1973); Chryssides 1991; Michael L. Mickler, A History of the Unification Church in America (1993).

figures.gloss: The movement's scripture, systematized from Moon's teaching by disciple Hyo Won Eu (Wolli Kangron, 1966; English 1973, from a 1952 manuscript core). It reads history as God's restoration providence: creation through dual characteristics (positive/negative, internal/external), the Fall as Lucifer's sexual seduction of Eve, and Jesus as achieving spiritual but not physical salvation — leaving a Second Advent, born in Korea, to complete the work.
figures.facet: A complete providential historiography: every biblical epoch is mapped onto numbered parallel periods converging on 20th-century Korea — the text does not supplement the Bible so much as re-key it around its reader's own time and place.
figures.provenance: Wolli Wonbon (1952 ms.); Wolli Haesol (1957); Exposition of the Divine Principle (Wolli Kangron, 1966; Eng. trans. 1973).

figures.gloss: The tradition's central sacrament: mass marriage ceremonies presided over by the True Parents, from the honored 36-couple cohort of 1960–61 to 30,000 couples in Seoul's Olympic Stadium (1992), with matches often arranged by Moon across national and racial lines. The preceding holy wine ceremony is held to graft the couple out of the fallen lineage into the True Parents' sinless one.
figures.facet: Initiation relocated into marriage: where most traditions transform the individual, the Blessing claims to transform a lineage — the ontological change runs through the couple's future children, making biology itself the ritual medium.
figures.provenance: Exposition of the Divine Principle (1966/1973); Chryssides 1991; Mickler 1993 (Blessing chronology per the movement's own widely reported counts).

figures.gloss: The law of restoration: what was lost through the Fall must be reclaimed by humans establishing 'conditions of indemnity' — fasting, celibate discipline, fundraising, witnessing, suffering accepted on behalf of ancestors and providence. God cannot simply forgive; restoration must be earned back through the same principled order by which it was lost.
figures.facet: Atonement stretched from a single event into a universal accounting system — every member's hardship is legible as payment on a cosmic debt, which gives the tradition both its famed work ethic and its critics' chief target.
figures.provenance: Exposition of the Divine Principle, Part II ch. 1 (1966/1973); Chryssides 1991.

edges.note: GOLD SEAM: Divine Principle holds that Jesus won spiritual salvation but was crucified before marrying; the True Parents claim to complete the physical restoration he left undone — a doctrinal graft directly onto Christology.
The text's hinge is a rereading of Genesis 3 — the Fall as Lucifer's sexual seduction of Eve — making misused love the root sin and the restored God-centered family its cure.

GOLD SEAM: the shamanic-Christian substrate of Korean new religions — Moon's revelatory spirit-world traffic and Korea-centered providence grew from the same soil where mudang practice met revivalist Christianity; shared milieu, not descent.

Indemnity generalizes atonement: where the Cross pays once for all, tanggam distributes restitution across every believer's acts — restoration by accumulated conditions rather than a single sacrifice.

dossier.essence: The Unification Church — founded in Seoul in 1954 as the Holy Spirit Association for the Unification of World Christianity, since 1994 operating mainly as the Family Federation for World Peace and Unification — is the Korean new religious movement built on Sun Myung Moon's claim that Jesus commissioned him (Easter 1935, self-reported) to complete the unfinished work of salvation. Its center is the doctrine that Moon and his wife Hak Ja Han are the True Parents, the restored Adam and Eve; the movement spread globally from Korea and Japan, with membership claims far exceeding scholarly estimates.
dossier.cosmos: In Divine Principle cosmology, God creates through dual characteristics — internal and external, positive and negative, masculine and feminine — toward the goal of God-centered families; the Fall was Lucifer's sexual seduction of Eve, corrupting the human lineage at its root. History is a providence of restoration mapped in numbered parallel periods converging on 20th-century Korea, where the Lord of the Second Advent completes the physical salvation the crucifixion left undone. Humans possess spirit selves that pass at death into a spirit world where growth continues, and the living can advance both their ancestors and themselves through conditions of indemnity.
dossier.practice: The tradition's central sacrament is the Blessing: mass wedding ceremonies presided over by the True Parents — from the 36-couple cohort of 1960–61 to 30,000 couples in Seoul's Olympic Stadium in 1992 — preceded by a holy wine ceremony held to graft the couple into the True Parents' sinless lineage. Members establish 'conditions of indemnity' through fasting, witnessing, fundraising, and accepted hardship, and the movement runs an extensive network of businesses, media, and civic organizations. Since Moon's death in 2012, Hak Ja Han has led the main body as True Mother, against schismatic churches founded by rival sons.
dossier.canon: Exposition of the Divine Principle (Wolli Kangron, 1966; English 1973), systematized from Moon's teaching by Hyo Won Eu, is the movement's scripture; Moon's collected speeches and the later anthology Cheon Seong Gyeong hold canonical weight in the Family Federation.
dossier.reflects: Messianism engineered as completion — a new revelation that grafts itself onto a host tradition's unfinished business and relocates salvation from the individual believer into marriage and lineage.
sources: Holy Spirit Association for the Unification of World Christianity, 'Divine Principle' (1966; Eng. trans. 1973)
George D. Chryssides, 'The Advent of Sun Myung Moon: The Origins, Beliefs and Practices of the Unification Church' (1991)
Eileen Barker, 'The Making of a Moonie: Choice or Brainwashing?' (1984)
